disease.name: Acute Respiratory Failure

emergencyPriority.notes: Life-threatening hypoxemia or hypercapnia requiring immediate airway assessment and intervention

mustNotMiss: Upper airway obstruction (stridor, foreign body, anaphylaxis)
Tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, distended neck veins, hypotension)
Massive pulmonary embolism (electromechanical dissociation, RV strain)
Cardiac tamponade (pulsus paradoxus, Kussmaul sign, muffled heart sounds)
Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell disease
Neuromuscular respiratory failure (ascending weakness, bulbar involvement)
Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (hemoptysis, falling hematocrit, BAL)
diagnosticClues: Type 1 respiratory failure: PaO2 < 60 mmHg with normal/low PaCO2 (V/Q mismatch, shunt)
Type 2 respiratory failure: PaCO2 > 50 mmHg with acidosis pH < 7.35 (hypoventilation)
PaO2/FiO2 ratio < 300 defines acute lung injury; < 200 defines ARDS per Berlin criteria
P(A-a)O2 gradient increased in V/Q mismatch and shunt; normal in hypoventilation
Response to 100% oxygen helps differentiate shunt (no response) from V/Q mismatch (response)
Respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation (HCO3 increases 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 rise)
exclusionClues: Normal ABG excludes acute respiratory failure but not chronic respiratory insufficiency
Normal chest X-ray makes pneumonia, pneumothorax, and pulmonary edema less likely
Negative D-dimer with low pre-test probability effectively excludes PE
BNP < 100 pg/mL makes cardiogenic pulmonary edema unlikely
Improvement with bronchodilators suggests reactive airway disease as primary cause
historyFeatures: Dyspnea onset: acute (minutes to hours) vs subacute (days) suggests etiology
Chest pain: pleuritic (PE, pneumonia) vs pressure (ACS, aortic dissection)
Cough: productive (pneumonia, bronchitis) vs dry (ILD, PE, pneumocystis)
Hemoptysis: massive vs scant (bronchiectasis, AV malformation, vasculitis)
Fever/chills suggest infection as cause of respiratory failure
Immune status: neutropenia, HIV, transplant, immunosuppressants
examFeatures: General: accessory muscle use, tracheal tug, intercostal retractions, paradoxical breathing
Airway: stridor (upper airway obstruction), gurgling (secretions), stertor (pharyngeal collapse)
Breathing: tachypnea, flail chest, asymmetric breath sounds, crackles, wheezes
Circulation: tachycardia, hypotension, JVD, gallop rhythm, pulsus paradoxus
Neurologic: confusion, agitation, somnolence, asterixis (CO2 narcosis)
riskFactors: Pre-existing lung disease (COPD, ILD, CF, asthma)
Neuromuscular disease (ALS, myasthenia gravis, Guillain-Barre, muscular dystrophy)
Recent surgery (especially thoracic, upper abdominal, cardiac)

subtypes.name: Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure (Type 1)
subtypes.pathophysiology: V/Q mismatch, intrapulmonary shunt, diffusion impairment, or hypoventilation

subtypes.name: Hypercapnic Respiratory Failure (Type 2)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Alveolar hypoventilation with or without increased dead space ventilation

subtypes.name: Peri-operative Respiratory Failure
subtypes.pathophysiology: Atelectasis, splinting from pain, diaphragmatic dysfunction, aspiration

subtypes.name: ARDS (Berlin Criteria)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Diffuse alveolar damage from direct or indirect lung injury, neutrophil-mediated inflammation

mimics: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema (heart failure, flash pulmonary edema)
Anxiety with hyperventilation syndrome (normal oxygenation, low PaCO2, alkalosis)
Chronic respiratory failure with acute decompensation (hypercapnia without acidosis)
Metabolic acidosis with compensatory tachypnea (Kussmaul breathing in DKA)
Pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure

investigations.detail: Essential for classification: Type 1 (low PaO2, low/normal PaCO2) vs Type 2 (high PaCO2, acidosis). Also assesses pH, HCO3, lactate, and A-a gradient.
Identify pneumonia, pulmonary edema, pneumothorax, effusion, atelectasis, or mass. Bilateral opacities in ARDS.
Characterizes parenchymal disease, detects occult pneumothorax, evaluates for PE, quantifies infiltrates.
Bedside TTE/TEE to assess LV/RV function, volume status, valvular disease, pulmonary hypertension, and exclude tamponade.
Flow-volume and pressure-volume loops assess patient-ventilator synchrony, auto-PEEP, airway resistance, and compliance.
Diagnostic for infection, DAH, aspiration, eosinophilic pneumonia, and organizing pneumonia.

pathophysiology.summary: Failure of the respiratory system to maintain adequate gas exchange due to impaired ventilation, oxygenation, or both. Type 1 results from V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment. Type 2 results from insufficient alveolar ventilation relative to CO2 production.
mechanisms: V/Q mismatch: most common cause of hypoxemia; regions of lung are ventilated but not perfused (dead space) or perfused but not ventilated (shunt-like)
Shunt: blood passes through non-ventilated alveoli (e.g., pneumonia, atelectasis); 100% O2 fails to correct PaO2
Hypoventilation: decreased minute ventilation increases PaCO2; corrected by increasing minute ventilation
Diffusion impairment: thickened alveolar-capillary membrane impairs O2 transfer; may improve with 100% O2

complications: Ventilator-associated lung injury (barotrauma, volutrauma, atelectrauma, biotrauma)
Oxygen toxicity (absorption atelectasis, tracheobronchitis, diffuse alveolar damage)

management.detail: CPAP or BiPAP for COPD exacerbation, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, and immunocompromised hypoxemia. Contraindications: altered mental status, hemodynamic instability, facial trauma, high aspiration risk.
Indications: NIV failure, apnea, severe hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, inability to protect airway. Settings: assist-control or SIMV, initial Vt 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight.
Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW), plateau pressure < 30 cmH2O, driving pressure < 15 cmH2O. Permissive hypercapnia accepted. Based on ARMA trial (NEJM 2000).
For severe ARDS with PaO2/FiO2 < 150. Improves oxygenation via V/Q matching and dorsal lung recruitment. Duration 16+ hours daily.

Head-of-bed elevation 30-45 degrees, oral chlorhexidine decontamination, daily sedation interruption and spontaneous breathing trials, DVT/PE prophylaxis, peptic ulcer prophylaxis, strict hand hygiene.

Neuromuscular blockade (cisatracurium) for severe ARDS with PaO2/FiO2 < 150 and refractory hypoxemia. Use train-of-four monitoring to guide dosing. Limit duration when possible. Based on ACURASYS trial (NEJM 2010).
VENovenous ECMO for severe ARDS with PaO2/FiO2 < 80 despite optimal conventional therapy. Consider EOLIA trial criteria and CESAR trial protocol.
